Name: information_theoretic_quantization
Title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Neural Network Quantization: A Rate-Distortion Approach
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fundamental limit of neural network quantization can be characterized using a rate-distortion framework, where the distortion measure is tied to the task-specific loss function. By optimizing the quantization scheme to minimize the distortion subject to a rate constraint, we can achieve a better trade-off between model size and accuracy.
Related Work: Recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation have explored the use of geometric and information-theoretic methods for neural network quantization and compression. However, these works focus on heuristic approaches and do not provide a fundamental limit on the achievable rate-distortion trade-off. Our work builds upon the ideas presented in Goal-Oriented 1-bit Quantization With Uncertain Distortion Measures and OACodec, which introduce risk measures and mutual information minimization for quantizer design and audio attribute disentanglement, respectively.
Abstract: Neural network quantization is a crucial step in deploying machine learning models on resource-constrained devices. However, the fundamental limits of quantization are not well understood. In this work, we propose a rate-distortion framework for neural network quantization, where the distortion measure is tied to the task-specific loss function. We derive a theoretical bound on the achievable rate-distortion trade-off and propose a quantization scheme that optimizes the rate-distortion trade-off. Our approach is based on a combination of information-theoretic and geometric methods, and we demonstrate its effectiveness on several benchmark datasets.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments on several benchmark datasets, including image classification and language modeling tasks. We will evaluate the performance of our proposed quantization scheme using metrics such as top-1 accuracy, bits per parameter, and computational resources required. We will also compare our approach with existing quantization schemes, including uniform quantization and learned quantization. Additionally, we will investigate the effect of different distortion measures and rate constraints on the achievable rate-distortion trade-off.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor is that the theoretical bound we derive may not be tight, and the proposed quantization scheme may not achieve the optimal rate-distortion trade-off. Another limitation is that our approach may require significant computational resources to optimize the quantization scheme, which could limit its applicability to large-scale models. Finally, our approach assumes a fixed distortion measure, which may not capture all aspects of the task-specific loss function.
